planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock after last frost in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; beach hibiscus is salt-spray tolerant and grows naturally along tropical coastlines worldwide, making it an excellent coastal garden shrub in zones 9b–12.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, coastal soil in full sun; beach hibiscus is a pantropical coastal shrub or small tree growing naturally at the landward edge of mangroves, sandy beaches, and coastal margins throughout the tropics. The large, round, heart-shaped leaves with their silvery, velvety undersides are distinctive and attractive, and the flowers are a rich yellow with a dark maroon center that changes to pink-red as the flowers age — the color change occurring within hours on the same flower. The wood is very buoyant and was traditionally used throughout the Pacific for outrigger canoe construction. Extremely salt-spray tolerant and useful for coastal hedging and screening. In zone 9, may be killed back by hard freezes but regrows from the base if roots are protected. The fibrous bark has traditional uses in making rope and cordage.

planting.note: Direct sow or plant container stock in spring in well-drained, sandy, saline-tolerant, neutral soil in full sun; portia tree is pantropical and highly salt-tolerant, growing naturally along tropical coastlines worldwide. In zone 9, protect from frost as young plants are more sensitive than established trees.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, saline-tolerant soil in full sun in tropical and near-tropical coastal areas; portia tree is a widely distributed pantropical beach and coastal tree in the hibiscus family, producing handsome, broadly heart-shaped leaves and large, yellow, hibiscus-like flowers with a dark maroon center that fade to purple-red as they age. Like beach hibiscus (Hibiscus tiliaceus), the flower color change occurs within hours on the same flower. The portia tree is one of the most salt-tolerant tropical trees available and has been widely planted throughout Pacific island communities for its high-quality, dense, decorative wood used in carving, furniture, and tool handles. The seeds, leaves, bark, and roots have various traditional medicinal applications across tropical Asia and Polynesia. In the US, suitable for south Florida and the warmest Gulf locations.

common_name: Wax Mallow (Turk's Cap, Mexican Turk's Cap)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in well-drained, average fertility, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wax mallow is a fast-growing native shrub of the Gulf Coastal Plain that blooms prolifically from summer through frost, tolerating drought and heat.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average fertility, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wax mallow (the Texas Turk's cap) is closely related to Malvaviscus arboreus but distinguished by flatter, less deeply lobed leaves. Like its relative, it produces characteristic semi-tubular, never fully-opening, bright red flowers throughout the long Gulf Coast growing season — from summer well into winter in mild years. The nectar-rich flowers are a critically important hummingbird nectar plant in the Gulf South, visited constantly by ruby-throated and rufous hummingbirds throughout their migration. The small red fruits are edible fresh and can be used for jelly. Extremely heat and drought tolerant once established; dies to the ground after hard freezes but regrows vigorously from the root in spring. An essential native plant for the hummingbird garden in zone 9a.

planting.note: Direct sow or plant container stock after last frost in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; scarlet rosemallow grows naturally along pond margins, wet meadows, and roadside ditches across the eastern and central US.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; scarlet rosemallow is a native wetland hibiscus of the eastern and central US, producing large, pink to pale lavender flowers with the distinctive dark maroon eye typical of the native rosemallows. The three-lobed, halberd-shaped (hastate) leaves distinguish this species from the broader-leaved H. moscheutos and H. grandiflorus. The plant grows naturally in wet to moist, open habitats — pond margins, wet meadows, and roadside ditches — and provides spectacular late-summer flowers that attract numerous native bumblebees. Like all native hibiscus, the seeds are eaten by wildlife and the leaves serve as larval food for several moth species. A bold, showy native for wet, sunny gardens and rain garden features in zones 5–9.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Caesar weed is a tropical annual that self-sows freely in zone 9 and can become weedy in disturbed areas. The fibrous stems have commercial fiber potential.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Caesar weed is a pantropical annual or short-lived perennial mallow widely distributed in disturbed habitats throughout the tropics and naturalized in the Gulf South. The lobed leaves and small, pink, hibiscus-like flowers are moderately attractive, and the plant provides useful wildlife habitat — the burr-like fruits attach to animal fur for seed dispersal. The fibrous stems produce a strong bast fiber historically used like jute in rope and coarse textile manufacture in parts of Africa and Asia. More notable as a botanical curiosity and fiber plant than as a primary garden ornamental; useful in wildlife gardens where its fruits provide seed food and the stems provide habitat. The plant is non-native but is naturalized throughout the Gulf South.
